title: Leveraged Funds and the Shadow Cost of Leverage Constraints
abstract: Using the most comprehensive data set of leveraged funds known to the literature, we measure the market-wide shadow cost of leverage constraints and examine its pricing implications. The shadow cost averages 0.53% per annum from 2006 to 2016, spikes upon quarter-ends when banks face tighter capital requirements, positively predicts future betting-against-beta (BAB) returns, and negatively correlates with contemporaneous BAB returns. Stocks that experience lower returns when the shadow cost increases earn 0.85% more per month. Overall, our shadow cost measure fits the predictions of leverage-constraint-based theories better than the widely used TED spread.

article.introduction:
We propose a new empiricalmeasure for the shadow cost of leverage constraints. The shadow cost measures the tightness of marginal investors' leverage constraints and is increasingly considered a key determinant of asset prices.1Examining the theoretical asset pricing implications of the shadow cost of leverage constraints requires an accurate empirical measure, which has remained elusive so far. As Garleanu and Pedersen (2011) demonstrate, the shadow cost is the difference between marginal investors' funding cost and the risk-free rate when the credit risk of the funding is zero (or fully hedged). Constructing an appropriate measure for the shadow cost therefore requires both data on funding rates, which are typically negotiated bilaterally (and confidentially) between leveraged investors and lenders, and adjustments for credit risk, which are potentially complicated. Due to these two empirical hurdles, the TED spread—the difference between three-month U.S. LIBOR and three-month Treasury bill rates—is widely used to capture the shadow cost of leverage constraints, despite the caveats that the LIBOR rate is not based on actual lending transactions and it is primarily a measure of banks' credit risk.2
Our key innovation is to construct a shadow cost measure based on the market price of leverage inferred from leveraged fund returns. As Frazzini and Pedersen (2012) emphasize, the sole purpose of leveraged funds is to generate levered returns on an underlying index. Given their passive portfolio choice, these funds' main task is to decide where and how they can obtain the lowest funding cost. To do so, leveraged funds borrow directly and simultaneously from multiple major broker-dealers to secure a competitive funding rate. Leveraged fund returns therefore incorporate the actual funding costs available to leveraged institutional investors in the market. Furthermore, leveraged funds borrow via derivative transactions that are marked-to-market daily with payments settled by a custodian bank, resulting in borrowing that has minimal exposure to credit risk. Consequently, using leveraged fund data to compute the shadow cost of leverage constraints circumvents the two aforementioned caveats associated with using the LIBOR rate.
Estimating the cost of leverage for leveraged funds is particularly straightforward given their unique feature of targeting aconstant dailyleverage. Frazzini and Pedersen (2012) use the return shortfall between leveraged funds' daily gross net asset value (NAV) returns and the target multiple of the underlying index returns to measure the cost of leverage. Building on their measure, we further subtract the corresponding return shortfall of unlevered index funds that track the same index—the counterfactual return shortfall when no leverage is used. Intuitively, this cost of leverage measure captures the additional cost of maintaining a constant leveraged exposure relative to the cost of maintaining an unlevered exposure.
At each point in time, we use the entire cross section of leveraged funds to infer the market-wide cost of leverage. To this end, we assemble the most comprehensive data set on leveraged funds known to the literature. This data set includes all leveraged funds, dead or active, since the launch of the first leveraged exchange-traded fund (ETF) in 2006. We then construct our aggregate cost of leverage measure as the cross-sectional median cost of leverage for all bullish leveraged funds.3We find that our cost of leverage measure tracks risk-free interest rates very closely, which is consistent with its characterization as a funding cost measure. Motivated by Garleanu and Pedersen (2011), we subsequently compute our shadow cost measure as the spread between our cost of leverage measure and the general collateral (GC) repo rate. We find that our shadow cost measure averages 0.53% per annum. It rose to2%per annum during the 2009 financial crisis and subsided after the U.S. government bailed out the financial and auto industries. Our shadow cost estimate is lower than the TED spread for most of the financial crisis period but higher afterward. While the two measures comove positively during the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis period, they exhibit very low correlation after the crisis.
Armed with this new shadow cost of leverage constraints measure, we use it and the TED spread to test four hypotheses from the Garleanu and Pedersen (2011) model. Specifically, the model predicts that (i) the shadow cost of leverage constraints is higher when leverage constraints are more binding, (ii) when the shadow cost increases, the required return of high-beta stocks relative to low-beta stocks decreases because the embedded leverage of high-beta stocks is more valuable, which consequently implies that (iii) high-beta stocks have contemporaneously higher returns, and finally (iv) exposure to time variation in the shadow cost is priced in the cross section of expected stock returns.4These predictions hold in other asset pricing models that feature leverage-constrained marginal investors.5
Our first set of tests examines the prediction in hypothesis (i). We posit that leverage constraints are likely to be more binding when market participants suffer large losses or when volatility is high. Empirically, we find that in these states, both our shadow cost measure and the TED spread are substantially higher, consistent with hypothesis (i). However, the increase in funding costs could be due to an increase in credit risk rather than a tighter leverage constraint. To focus on the latter effect, we conduct a sharper test of hypothesis (i) by examining changes in both our shadow cost measure and the TED spread at quarter-ends, when banks' capital constraints are likely to be more binding while their credit risks stay the same.6We find that our shadow cost measures increase significantly by 59 basis points (bps), on an annualized basis, at quarter-ends. In contrast, the corresponding changes in the TED spread are minimal and insignificant.
Our second set of tests examines hypotheses (ii) and (iii), which are derived from the theoretical prediction that the shadow cost of leverage constraints explains the betting-against-beta (BAB) effect. To test hypothesis (ii), we regress BAB portfolio returns over the next month on the lagged moving averages of our shadow cost measure. We find that our shadow cost measure predicts the BAB factor of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) with a positive coefficient that is consistent with hypothesis (ii). In robustness tests, we use alternative BAB portfolios constructed in Cohen, Polk, and Vuolteenaho (2005) and Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018) and find that our shadow cost measure positively predicts equal-weighted BAB returns but not value-weighted BAB returns. In contrast, we find that the TED spread predicts future BAB returns with a theoretically incorrect negative sign, in line with findings of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014). Our results remain qualitatively the same when we control for the TED spread, the leverage constraint tightness (LCT) measure of Boguth and Simutin (2018), and the margin debt (MD) measure of Asness et al. (2020).
We find stronger evidence in support of the leverage-constraint-based explanation of the BAB effect when we test hypothesis (iii). We find that the contemporaneous correlation between our shadow cost measure and BAB returns is negative and highly statistically significant. Furthermore, in contrast to our predictive results, this robust negative correlation holds whether we use the BAB factor of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) or various alternative BAB portfolios. This negative relation is also robust to controlling for the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), market returns, and the TED spread.
Related to our second set of tests, we use our measure to better understand the relation between the LCT measure proposed by Boguth and Simutin (2018) and the BAB premium. Boguth and Simutin (2018) argue that their LCT measure—the aggregate market beta of active mutual funds' holdings (hereafter, we refer to the LCT measure andβMFinterchangeably)—is a proxy for the implicit leverage taken by leverage-constrained mutual funds. Higher demand for leverage from mutual funds leads to a higherβMF, a tighter leverage constraint, and a higher BAB premium. We highlight that the positive correlation betweenβMFand the BAB premium is predicated on the assumption that mutual funds' demand for leverage drives the equilibrium. This correlation turns negative when the supply of leverage is driving the equilibrium, that is, a higher supply of leverage loosens the leverage constraint, leading to a lower BAB premium, and thus, makes holding high-beta stocks more attractive to mutual funds (i.e., a higherβMF). To test this prediction, we combine our shadow cost measure andβMFas a price-quantity pair to identify the demand and supply shifts. Consistent with our argument, we find that the correlation betweenβMFand future BAB returns is more negative when supply shifts dominate.
Our final set of tests examines the implications of leverage constraints on the cross section of stock returns. We run rolling regressions of excess stock returns on market excess returns and the innovations in our shadow cost measure. We find strong evidence that exposure to time variation in our shadow cost measure negatively predicts stock returns in the cross section, supporting hypothesis (iv). In contrast, we find no evidence that exposure to the TED spread is priced. A zero-investment portfolio that is long the top-quintile portfolio (i.e., stocks that outperform when the shadow cost is high) and short the bottom-quintile portfolio generates average value-weighted future excess returns of−0.85%per month, a Fama-French three-factor alpha of−0.81%per month, and a Fama-French five-factor alpha of−0.55%per month. Further analysis shows that the risk premium of this zero-investment portfolio cannot be explained by exposures to other proxies for funding conditions and does not cluster in the crisis period. Finally, we find that this zero-investment portfolio helps explain more than40%of the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) alpha of the BAB factor.
Our main contribution is to provide an attractive alternative to the widely used TED spread for measuring the shadow cost of leverage constraints. Ex-ante, our measure offers several advantages over the TED spread. First, given that the shadow cost of leverage constraints can arise independent from credit risk (Garleanu and Pedersen (2011)), leveraged funds provide a unique empirical setting to study the shadow cost of leverage constraints when the complication from credit risk is minimal. In contrast, the TED spread largely measures the credit risk of unsecured loans to large commercial banks. Second, the LIBOR rate is indicative in nature and can be substantially lower than banks' marginal cost of capital when banks' capital constraints bind (Kisin and Manela (2016)). In contrast, our measure captures the actual cost of leverage experienced by leveraged funds. Ex-post, our empirical findings suggest that our measure of the shadow cost of leverage constraints fits the predictions of leverage-constraint-based theories better than the TED spread.
Our paper relates to several strands of literature. First, Koijen and Yogo (2016), Kisin and Manela (2016), and Fleckenstein and Longstaff (2020) explicitly quantify the shadow cost of capital for market participants. Our shadow measure is more applicable to leveraged equity investors, as most of the leveraged funds in our sample track equity indices. We further use our shadow cost measure to test the time-series and cross-sectional pricing implications of leverage constraints in the stock market.
Second, recent empirical research uses a variety of proxies for the tightness of leverage constraints to investigate the important pricing implications of leverage constraints in the equity market, such as the TED spread in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014), the ratio of MD over total market capitalization for NYSE stocks in Asness et al. (2020), the aggregate market beta of active mutual funds in Boguth and Simutin (2018), the leverage of security broker-dealers in Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014), and the equity capital ratio of primary dealers in He, Kelly, and Manela (2017).7All of these measures except the TED spread are “quantity” measures that correspond to the level of leverage, which is fundamentally distinct from a “price” measure that gauges the tightness of leverage constraints like our shadow cost measure. While the level of leverage is in itself interesting, the pricing implications of intermediary asset pricing theories are ultimately driven by the tightness of leverage constraints. Empirically, we find that the time-series and cross-sectional pricing effects of our shadow cost measure are robust to controlling for each of these existing measures.
Finally, our paper contributes to the literature on leveraged funds by assembling a comprehensive data set that we believe includes all leveraged funds, dead and active, that track a constant multiple of daily index returns.8Frazzini and Pedersen (2012) study the pricing of embedded leverage in different financial instruments including seven leveraged funds. Tang and Xu (2013) find that the interest rate is the most important determinant of the deviation between daily fund returns and target returns using 12 leveraged funds between 2006 and 2011. Tuzun (2014) and Jiang and Yan (2020) investigate a large panel of leveraged ETFs, collected from Morningstar Direct and Bloomberg. In comparison, our data set includes not only leveraged ETFs but also open-end mutual funds, resulting in two to four times as many funds.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we provide institutional background on leveraged funds. In SectionII, we describe the theory behind and the empirical setup for our key empirical measure. SectionIIIdescribes our comprehensive daily leveraged fund data set and examines our empirical estimates of the cost of leverage and the shadow cost of leverage constraints. SectionsIVtoVItest hypotheses (i) to (iv) using our shadow cost measure and the TED spread. We conclude in SectionVII.
article.I. Institutional Background:
The leveraged funds in our sample are open-end mutual funds and ETFs that aim to track a multiple of benchmark index returns, before fees and expenses. Although a handful of leveraged and inverse mutual funds first emerged in the United States in 1993, the number of leveraged funds increased substantially after ProShares offered the first leveraged (and inverse) ETFs in 2006. Demand for leveraged funds has been on the rise because leveraged funds offer a convenient, transparent, and potentially less expensive way to obtain leverage. Using our comprehensive data set, we find that the number of leveraged funds has grown from 127 to around 400, and the exposure value has grown from $10 billion to over $75 billion, between 2006 and 2016. Most leveraged funds are managed by four investment companies, Rydex, Profunds, Direxion, and ProShares, with the former two focusing on leveraged mutual funds and the latter two focusing on leveraged ETFs. To attract more assets under management, leveraged funds focus on finding the benchmark to which investors demand leveraged exposure and the cheapest way to achieve such leveraged exposure.
Figure1illustrates a typical leveraged fund using the official webpage for ProShares Ultra Financials. The investment objective, highlighted in bold at the top, states that “[The fund] seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to two times (2×) the daily performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Financials Index.” The fund emphasizes that the promised leverage multiple pertains only to daily returns based on the daily NAV and explicitly warns that returns over periods other than one day can differ from two times the benchmark returns. Accordingly, our daily returns are based on NAV, which in this example is calculated by JPMorgan Chase Bank, National Association at 4:00 pm ET and reflects the fundamental value of securities owned by the fund.
A snapshot of ProShares Ultra Financials' daily holdings in Panel A of Figure1demonstrates how the 2×leverage is achieved. The total market value of this fund is $906,931,618. The fund obtains exposure two times its market value by investing $762,011,691 in the constituents of the Dow Jones U.S. Financials Index and acquiring the remaining exposure of $1,050,292,778 via equity total return swaps.9
In general, equity swaps are the most common derivative used by leveraged funds to achieve their target leverage multiple.10In such transactions, leveraged funds are the equity return leg receiver and the floating interest rate leg payer, effectively borrowing from their swap counterparties. The interest rate leg is typically the LIBOR rate plus a markup, with the rate updating daily to reduce interest rate exposure. The equity return leg is typically the total return of the index from the initial price specified in the swap contract. These bilateral swap contracts can be terminated and opened daily without penalty by either party. Consequently, swap counterparties provide competitive funding rates as leveraged funds constantly look for the cheapest way to obtain the target risk exposure.
The credit risk of these swap transactions is extremely low because the swaps are marked to market daily and the collateral and margin payment required are maintained in a separate account controlled by the custodian bank of the leveraged funds. To reduce counterparty risk and obtain competitive financing rates, leveraged funds typically enter swap contracts with multiple major global financial institutions simultaneously. For example, Panel B of Figure1shows that ProShares Ultra Financials' swap counterparties include UBS, Deutsche Bank, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Société Générale, Citibank, Goldman Sachs, and Credit Suisse, which collectively represent the largest broker-dealers in the market.
The discussion so far helps illustrate why using leveraged funds' cost of leverage circumvents the two empirical problems associated with using the LIBOR rate, as it proxies directly for the funding costs experienced by leveraged investors and contains a minimal credit risk premium. These institutional details also motivate our use of the median cost of leverage experienced by leveraged funds to proxy for the market price of leverage available to the marginal investor. We stress that this is different from assuming that leveraged funds are the marginal investor, whose Euler equation determines asset prices. Our assumption is that leveraged funds' cost of leverage proxies for the prevailing fund cost for leveraged institutional investors, which is reasonable given that both leveraged funds and marginal investors such as hedge funds borrow directly from the same broker-dealers and leveraged funds search for the best financing terms daily in this leverage market.
article.II. Model:
To the extent that leverage-constraint-based predictions are specific to the pure financing cost of levered positions, we should subtractγαjt1xfromαitif the trading fee minus the security lending income experienced by leveraged funds isγtimes that experienced by unlevered funds. Our main specification of equation (4) usesγ=1. This choice is motivated by (i) our conversations with industry practitioners that leveraged funds on average earn only slightly more securities lending income than an unlevered index fund with the same assets under management, (ii) the fact that the swap contracts used by leveraged funds are opened and closed at the daily market close price without additional fees beyond the financing spread already specified in the contracts, and (iii) the straightforward interpretation of equation (4) being the additional cost of maintaining a levered position relative to an unlevered position. More generally,γcan vary between 1 andδ. As a sensitivity check, we computeritcin the extreme case ofγ=δby subtractingδαjt1xrather thanαjt1xin equation (4).11We find qualitatively similar results, which for brevity we report in TablesIA.IIthroughIA.VIIIof the Internet Appendix.12
To the extent that leveraged funds' borrowing is risk free, the spread between the uncollateralized rate and the collateralized risk-free rate equals the shadow cost of leverage constraints for risk-tolerant investors, who are also the marginal investor in the model.
Motivated by equation (9), our shadow cost of leverage constraint measure is the difference between our cost of leverage measure and the (collateralized) risk-free rate.13We now turn to the data and empirical tests using our shadow cost measure and the TED spread.
article.III. Data and Empirical Estimates:
In this section, we describe our new data set of leveraged funds and use it to estimate the cost of leverage and the shadow cost of leverage constraints.
Our sample period starts in June 2006 when the first leveraged ETF is offered and ends in December 2016. For our research purpose, we start by constructing a comprehensive data set that comprises all leveraged funds, dead or alive, that track a constant multiple of the daily performance of an underlying benchmark index. In constructing this list, we rely primarily on the Center for Research in Security Prices U.S. Survivor-Bias-Free Mutual Fund Database (CRSP MFDB) because it includes dead funds and thus is free of survivorship bias. We complement the CRSP data with leveraged funds retrieved from Bloomberg, because neither CRSP MFDB nor Bloomberg provide a direct identifier for leveraged funds.14In our Internet Appendix SectionI, we provide a step-by-step description of how we construct the comprehensive list of daily leveraged funds.
We obtain most of the fund-level information, such as fund returns, total net assets, and expense ratios, from CRSP MFDB. We manually collect the historical leverage multiple and the name of the underlying index from Bloomberg and SEC filings. We obtain the daily total returns on the underlying indexes from Bloomberg. To ensure the accuracy of the funding costs we compute, we only include domestic funds that track clearly defined and tradable spot indexes.15FigureIA.1of the Internet Appendix presents the number of funds and the exposure value across leverage levels in our data set over time. The most popular leverage level for bullish (bearish) funds over most of the sample period based on the number of funds is 2×(−2×). The number of 3×(−3×) funds, which show up in late 2009, grew to about 40.0% (41.5%) of the number of 2×(−2×) funds by the end of 2016.
Our final data set contains both leveraged ETFs and leveraged mutual funds, but not leveraged exchange-traded notes. In this paper, we focus on bullish funds, which comprise a total of 308,015 fund-day observations between 2006 and 2016. Since our leveraged funds data set is new to the literature, we report detailed summary statistics for the variables used to construct our cost of leverage measure next.
Panel A of TableIreports summary statistics for the variables used to compute the cost of leverage. We first look at expense ratios. The column labeled “ExpRatioit¯” reports the cross-sectional average of the time-series mean of the expense ratios for funds within a target leverage level (δ). The average expense ratio ranges from0.95%per annum for 3×funds to2.09%per annum for 1.5×funds. The 3×funds have the lowest average expense ratios because they are mostly ETFs offered by Proshares and Direxion, which charge lower fees. The column “σi(ExpRatioit)¯” reports the cross-sectional average of the time-series standard deviation of the expense ratio. The average standard deviation of the expense ratio is about two orders of magnitude smaller than the average expense ratio. For example, 2×funds have an expense ratio of1.27%per annum with a standard deviation of0.02%per annum on average. This indicates that expense ratios change infrequently over time in our sample.
We next examine whether leveraged funds achieve the target multiple of the benchmark's daily performance. We estimate the average realized leverage multiple by computing the ordinary least squares (OLS) regression betas from regressing daily returns on a leveraged fund on the corresponding benchmark returns. The column “δ̂i¯” reports the realized leverage multiple averaged over funds for each target leverage level. We find that the average realized leverage is equal to the target leverage up to the second decimal place across the board. Furthermore, the column “σ(δ̂i)” finds limited cross-sectional variation in the realized leverage within each of the target leverage levels, ranging between 0.002 for 1.5×funds and 0.01 for the other funds. Finally, the column “Ri2¯” reports the cross-sectional average ofRi2≡1−V(αit)V(rit), which measures how much of the variation in daily fund returns can be explained by the target multiple of the underlying index (see the definition ofαitin equation (2)). We find that theR2s average99.94%across all funds, ranging from99.66%for 2.5×funds to99.95%for 2×funds with an overall cross-sectional standard deviation of0.10%. These results together suggest that leveraged funds' returns are on average very close to the target-levered index returns.
Nevertheless, the small difference between the realized leverage and the target leverage introduces substantial noise to our cost of leverage measure.16The cross-sectional average of the time-series standard deviation ofαit, reported in the column “σi(αit)¯,” indicates large daily variation inαit. For example, the average daily variation inαitfor 2×funds is11.98%per annum, a number that exceeds what we expect for realistic daily variation in funding costs. This is because the volatility of daily index returns is far larger than the volatility of funding costs, so that even a small difference between the realized leverage and the target leverage, multiplied by highly volatile index returns, results in substantial noise in our cost of leverage measure at the fund level.
We start our empirical analysis by comparingrcto two benchmark risk-free rates: the overnight Treasury GC repo rate and the four-week Treasury bill (one-month T-bill) rate. All interest rates and interest rate spreads are in percentage points per annum (see our Internet Appendix SectionIIfor detailed descriptions of our annualization procedures). Panel A of Figure2illustrates the time-series relation by plotting the three-month moving averages. Consistent with the view thatrcis a cost of leverage measure, we find thatrctracks the benchmark risk-free rates closely. For example, when the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered the fed funds rate rapidly from 2007 to 2009 to combat the financial crisis,rcdeclined in tandem.
In Panel B of Figure2, we plot the three-month moving average of our shadow cost estimate. We observe that the shadow cost is noticeably lower than the TED spread for most of the financial crisis period, but generally higher than the TED spread after the crisis. Our interpretation is that while leveraged funds generally have to pay a spread over the LIBOR rate, during the crisis, their financing rates are less affected by the rising credit risk in the unsecured interbank loan market as measured by the TED spread. This is because leveraged funds' borrowing has minimal credit risk, as discussed in SectionI. Nevertheless, for several months around Lehman Brothers' collapse in September 2008, the shadow cost rose faster than and eventually exceeded the TED spread. It peaked at around2%per annum, before falling together with the TED spread when the government bailed out the banking and auto industries. Overall, Panel B of Figure2shows that the shadow cost of leverage constraints and credit risk possess different dynamics, although they are positively correlated during crisis periods.
Panel B of TableIreports summary statistics for our estimates of the cost of leverage and the shadow cost of leverage constraints. For comparison, we also report summary statistics for the benchmark interest rates and the TED spread. We first look at the average value of these measures in our sample period. We find thatrcis about0.48%per annum higher than the EFFR, 0.53% higher than the GC repo rate, and0.69%higher than the one-month T-bill rate. These numbers indicate that on average leveraged funds borrow at a rate higher than the overnight uncollateralized borrowing rate for depository institutions (EFFR), which is, in turn, higher than the overnight collateralized borrowing rate (GC repo rate) and the U.S. government funding cost (one-month T-bill rate). Using the GC repo rate as the benchmark risk-free rate, our baseline estimate of the shadow cost is on average 0.53% per annum. However, this specification may overestimate the shadow cost if the convenience yield on the Treasury securities used as repo collateral drives the GC repo rate below the true collateralized risk-free rate (Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2012)). To account for this possibility, we use the EFFR as an alternative benchmark risk-free rate to compute the shadow cost, which results in an average estimate of0.48%per annum.18This is a lower bound on our shadow cost estimate because the EFFR is an unsecured lending rate that is likely to be higher than the true collateralized risk-free rate. In comparison, Kisin and Manela (2016) estimate that the shadow cost of banks' tier 1 risk-based capital ratio requirement is on average0.30%per annum, and Fleckenstein and Longstaff (2020) estimate an average shadow cost of balance sheet constraints of0.81%per annum for intermediaries trading in the Treasury note cash and futures markets.19Koijen and Yogo (2016) find that the shadow cost of capital for life insurers can be as high as $0.96 per dollar of statutory capital during the financial crisis.
We find that aggregating both cross-sectionally and over time greatly reduces the standard deviation of our cost of leverage measure. First, taking the cross-sectional median reduces the impact of fund-specific tracking errors. The daily standard deviation of the cross-sectional median cost of leverage (rc) is3.83%per annum, which is much lower than the average standard deviation of the fund-level cost of leverage measure (see columnσi(αit)¯in TableIPanel A). Next, using the monthly average value further mitigates the impact of the noise in the daily time series. We find that the monthlyrc, the cross-sectional median of the monthly fund-level cost of leverage, has a first-order autoregressive (AR1) coefficient of 0.83. This AR1 coefficient is much higher than the coefficient of−0.03for the dailyrcand is more in line with the AR1 coefficients of the benchmark interest rates. Similarly, the monthlyψhas less extreme maximum and minimum values than the daily version, with an interquartile range of0.40%per annum, which is a reasonable range for the shadow cost of leverage constraints. Overall, these results help alleviate concerns that measurement errors may bias against finding significant results.
Finally, since our data set includes both leveraged ETFs and mutual funds, we examine whether theψs estimated from the ETF sample and the mutual fund sample are different. In TableIA.Iof the Internet Appendix, we run a panel regressions of the fund-levelψon an ETF indicator with separate day and leverage fixed effects. We find that ETF-basedψis on average 42 bps lower thanψbased on mutual funds. To understand this difference, we sequentially include additional fixed effects in the panel regression. We find that after we control for day-leverage-index fixed effects, the coefficient on the ETF dummy drops to around 12 bps, which is statistically insignificant. When we further control for size (i.e., the log of the fund's total net assets), we find an even smaller and still insignificant difference of around 8 bps. Therefore, 34 bps out of the 42 bp difference inψestimated from ETFs and mutual funds can be attributed to the different daily composition of the two samples in terms of fund size, the underlying index, and the target leverage level. The remaining 8 bps may be due to the fund type. For example, the creation/redemption mechanism of ETFs can help lower the cost of maintaining a levered position. Given that our goal is to test the theoretical implications of the market-wide shadow cost of leverage and the noise in the fund-levelψ, we focus on the medianψestimated from the full sample and do not attempt to model the cross-sectional heterogeneity. Empirically, the medianψestimated using the full sample has correlation of 0.97 with the first principal component of the medianψs estimated from ETFs and mutual funds.
We now use our shadow cost measure to test the implications of leverage constraints as posited in hypotheses (i) to (iv) listed in the introduction.
article.IV. Do Shadow Cost Proxies Capture the T:
We first examine the prediction in hypothesis (i) that the shadow cost is higher in periods in which leverage constraints are likely to be more binding. We separate months into low, middle, and high states based on the 10thand 90thpercentiles of the market return (MKT) and VIX, respectively. Theories featuring an equity constraint such as those in He and Krishnamurthy (2013) and Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014) predict that the constraint is more binding when market participants suffer a large loss. Theories featuring a debt constraint such as those in Brunnermeier (2009) and Adrian and Shin (2014) predict that the constraint is more binding when market volatility increases. Accordingly, we expect the shadow cost estimate to be highest in both the low state of MKT and the high state of VIX.
Panel A of TableIIreports the average value for our shadow cost measure within each MKT state. We find that our shadow cost estimateψaverages0.82%in the low MKT state, versus0.45%and0.40%in the middle and high states, respectively. Furthermore, based on a two-samplet-test for equal means, there is no statistical difference (p-value = 0.81) in the average shadow cost estimate between the high market state and the middle market state. Theories typically predict the leverage constraint to be more binding in bear markets, but are ambiguous regarding the tightness of leverage constraints in bull markets. This is because both the demand for and the supply of leverage can increase in bull markets, and depending on which force dominates, the leverage constraint can be either tighter or looser in bull market states than in the middle market state.
For comparison, Panel A of TableIIalso presents the monthly average for alternative funding condition proxies used in prior studies conditional on different MKT states. We find that the TED spread, the leverage (LEV) measure of Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014), and the MD measure of Asness et al. (2020) have the highest (lowest) average value in the low (high) MKT state. In contrast, the intermediary capital ratio (ICR) measure of He, Kelly, and Manela (2017) and the LCT measure of Boguth and Simutin (2018) do not exhibit a monotonic relation with the market states.20
Panel B of TableIIrepeats the tests using VIX to define market states. We find thatψand TED have the highest average value in the high VIX state, while ICR has the lowest value in the high VIX state, all of which are consistent with the leverage constraint being more binding in high VIX months.21In contrast, LEV and MD are not monotonically related to the market states defined by VIX. The LCT measure has the lowest value in the high VIX state, contrary to the intuition that the leverage constraint is more binding in these months. This puzzling pattern is also noted by Boguth and Simutin (2018).
Whileψand the TED spread being higher during periods of low market returns and high VIX is consistent with hypothesis (i), these patterns are also consistent with alternative explanations. For instance, because low MKT and high VIX states cluster in the crisis period, higher funding costs can stem from a higher compensation for credit risk rather than a tighter leverage constraint. We therefore conduct a sharper test of hypothesis (i) by examining changes inψand the TED spread upon quarter-ends. The idea is that while the credit risk associated with lending is stable around quarter-ends, financial intermediaries subject to quarter-end reporting are less willing to lend their balance sheet, resulting in a tighter leverage constraint. Furthermore, quarter-ends are evenly distributed over time, offering an opportunity to check whether the results are driven by the financial crisis.
In column (1) of Panel A, we find that the regression coefficient onQtrEndis 0.59 (t-statistic=2.82), indicating that the shadow cost of leverage constraints as captured byψis0.59%per annum higher at quarter-ends. Column (2) adds an indicator variable for month-ends that are not quarter-ends as a placebo test. The idea is that the leverage constraint should not tighten on month-ends when banks are not required to report snapshots of their financial positions. We find a statistically insignificant loading on the nonquarter-end month-end indicator variable with at-statistic of 0.46, while the loading onQtrEndremains highly significant (t-statistic=2.83). These results suggest that the increase inψis not a monthly effect but rather specific to quarter-ends. We conduct a robustness test in column (3) by further controlling for the autocorrelation structure by adding the lagged dependent variable. The loading onQtrEndincreases to 81 bps per annum (t-statistic=3.15), while the loading on the nonquarter-end month-end indicator remains insignificant.
For comparison, column (4) examines the quarter-end change in the TED spread using the same regression specification as that of column (3). The regression coefficient onQtrEndis−0.01(t-statistic =−1.50), suggesting that the quarter-end change in the TED spread is close to zero. Column (5) conducts a similar analysis on the quarter-end change in the GC repo rate and finds a statistically significant but economically small change of 3 bps upon quarter-ends. The small change in the repo rate is consistent with the findings by Munyan (2017) that while banks subject to quarter-end reporting reduce their repo borrowing upon quarter-ends, the excess repo lending supply is absorbed by Fed facilities such as the overnight reverse repo program.
Finally, to examine whether the results are driven by the financial crisis, Panel B of TableIIIreports the averageψon quarter-ends and nonquarter-end days by year. We find thatψis higher upon quarter-ends in seven of the 11 years in our sample period.
Overall, the significant increase in quarter-endψ, contrasted with the lack of movement in the quarter-end TED spread, indicates thatψis a more accurate measure of the shadow cost of leverage constraints. Our result echoes the finding by Du, Tepper, and Verdelhan (2018) that currency derivative contracts exhibit greater deviations from covered interest rate parity when the contracts straddle quarter-ends, indicating that the financing of arbitrage positions during quarter-ends is more costly. Both our finding and the finding in Du, Tepper, and Verdelhan (2018) suggest that the LIBOR rate (and therefore the TED spread) can underestimate financial intermediaries' marginal cost of capital when banks' capital constraints bind.
article.V. Relation to BAB Returns:
In this section, we examine hypotheses (ii) and (iii), which state that when the shadow cost increases, we should observe higher future BAB returns and lower contemporaneous BAB returns. Both hypotheses are derived from the prediction that a higher shadow cost decreases the required return for high-beta stocks relative to that of low-beta stocks. We also contrast our shadow cost measure with the LCT measure proposed by Boguth and Simutin (2018), which is also used to predict the BAB premium.
We use the BAB factor of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) as the benchmark portfolio to test the leverage-constraint-based explanation of the BAB effect. Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018) note that there are three “unconventional” procedures in constructing the BAB factor of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014), namely, rank-weighting portfolios, hedging by (de)leveraging, and the nonstandard beta estimation. We therefore conduct robustness tests using the equal-weighted and value-weighted beta arbitrage portfolios constructed in Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018). These two beta arbitrage portfolios go long (short) the top (bottom) tercile of stocks based on preformation OLS betas estimated over a five-year window using daily stock returns and then hedge the market exposure using the market tilt estimated over a one-year window based on postformation portfolio returns. While these two beta arbitrage portfolios are constructed using different portfolio weighting, hedging, and beta estimation schemes from the original BAB factor, conceptually, they still capture the BAB premium. We therefore estimate nine regressions in total for each combination of the three BAB factors andn.
Columns (1) to (3) in Panel A of TableIVshow that the 1-, 6-, and 12-monthψall predict the benchmark BAB return with the theoretically correct positive sign. The regression coefficients are larger when using the 6- and 12-month predictors, witht-statistics of 1.85 and 2.16, respectively. Correcting for the finite-sample size distortion, theset-statistics correspond to bootstrapp-values of 0.12 and 0.10, respectively. These coefficients are economically meaningful because, for example, the coefficient of 1.56 on the six-month moving average ofψmeans that a 1 percentage point increase in the averageψover the past six months is associated with a 1.56 percentage point increase in the next month's BAB premium. Columns (4) to (6) in Panel A of TableIVshow that the 1-, 6-, and 12-monthψalso predict the equal-weighted beta arbitrage return (EWβArb)with the theoretically correct positive sign. The predictive coefficients are similar to the corresponding coefficients in columns (1) to (3) and the associated bootstrapp-values are 0.20, 0.07, and 0.08, respectively. In contrast, columns (7) through (9) show thatψdoes not predict the value-weighted beta arbitrage factor of Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018). The predictive coefficients are all insignificantly different from zero, with bootstrapp-values all larger than 0.45.22
In Panel B of TableIV, we control for the variables known to predict BAB returns, including the TED spread used in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014), the LCT measure of Boguth and Simutin (2018), and the MD measure of Asness et al. (2020). For brevity, we report only the results using the six-month moving averages of the predictors.23
Specifications (1) to (3) run bivariate regressions of the next month's BAB return onψand the TED spread. We find that the TED spread predicts all three BAB portfolios with a negative coefficient that is statistically significant at the 10% level. As noted by Frazzini and Pedersen (2014), this is opposite the positive association between the shadow cost of leverage constraints and the BAB premium predicted by hypothesis (ii). After controlling for the TED spread, the coefficients onψare slightly smaller than those in the univariate regression results but exhibit the same pattern:ψpredicts the one-month-ahead benchmark BAB and EWβArbreturn with coefficients of 1.50 (p-value=0.07) and 2.07 (p-value=0.07), but does not predict VWβArb.
Next, specifications (4) to (6) control for the LCT measure rather than the TED spread. Boguth and Simutin (2018) find that the LCT measure strongly positively predicts future BAB returns in their sample period. When controlling for the LCT measure,ψpredicts the benchmark BAB return with a coefficient of 1.07 (p-value=0.27) and the EWβArbwith a coefficient of 2.15 (p-value=0.04). Similar to previous results,ψdoes not predict VWβArb. When controlling forψ, the LCT measure does not predict any of the three BAB portfolio returns in our sample period. We investigate this issue further in SectionV.C.
In specifications (7) to (9), we control for the MD measure. Asness et al. (2020) find that MD negatively predicts BAB returns and interpret low MD as an indication of a tighter leverage constraint. Consistent with their findings, the bivariate regressions from (7) and (8) show that MD negatively predicts the benchmark BAB return and EWβArbreturn in the next month, although the coefficient is only significant for EWβArb. Similar toψ, MD does not predict the VWβArbreturn. After controlling for MD, the coefficients onψare 2.03 (p-value=0.10), 3.34 (p-value=0.005), and−1.17(p-value=0.50) for predicting benchmark BAB, EWβArb, and VWβArb, respectively.
Finally, in TableIA.Xof our Internet Appendix, we follow Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018) by including controls for the time-varying market exposure of the BAB portfolios. These specifications yield a slightly more positive relation betweenψand the future BAB portfolio returns. Overall, when testing for the positive association between the shadow cost of leverage constraints and the BAB premium as predicted by hypothesis (ii), we find supportive evidence when using the original BAB factor and the equal-weighted BAB portfolios, but not when using the value-weighted BAB portfolios. We emphasize that one should be exercise caution in interpreting return predictability regressions over short samples. Therefore, when evaluating the support for the leverage-constraint-based explanation of the BAB effect, we put more weight on our tests of the negative contemporaneous relation betweenψand BAB returns in the next section.
In specification (1) of TableV, we regress the BAB returns on contemporaneousΔψand market excess returns. We find that the regression coefficient onΔψis−0.04and the Newey-Westt-statistic with 22 lags is−3.84. In regression specification (2), we control for potential serial correlations by adding the lagged dependent variable to specification (1) and find little change in the negative contemporaneous relation betweenΔψand BAB returns. In specification (3), we additionally control for the change in the TED spread, which Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) find is negatively correlated with contemporaneous BAB returns. After controlling for the change in the TED spread, there remains a robust negative contemporaneous relation betweenΔψand BAB returns, with a coefficient on BAB returns of−0.04and at-statistic of−3.91. In specification (4), we control for volatility risk by including changes in the daily VIX and find a similar negative contemporaneous relation betweenΔψand BAB returns. In specification (5), we simultaneously control for the change in the TED spread, the change in VIX, the market excess return, and the lagged BAB. The contemporaneous correlation betweenΔψand BAB returns remains negative and statistically significant.
Finally, in the last two columns, we replace the dependent variable with EWβArband VWβArbreturns, while keeping all the control variables in specification (5). We find that both EWβArband VWβArbreturns have a negative and statistically significant correlation withΔψ. In TableIA.XIof our Internet Appendix, we find qualitatively the same contemporaneous results when we follow Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018) and include controls for the time-varying market exposure of the BAB portfolios. The change in the TED spread is also negatively correlated with the EWβArband VWβArbreturns, but only the correlation with the EWβArbreturn is statistically significant.
Therefore, in contrast to the test results for hypothesis (ii), we find consistent evidence in support of hypothesis (iii) whether we use the BAB factor, the EWβArbportfolio, or the VWβArbportfolio. There are two potential explanations for this finding. The first is that tests based on the contemporaneous correlation are more powerful and have better finite-sample properties than the predictive regressions, since discount rate news (i.e., revisions in expected future returns) constitute a greater proportion of realized returns than expected returns. The other possibility is that while both equal- and value-weighted BAB portfolios are affected by the shadow cost of leverage constraints, they may have different exposures to the other risk factors (see, for example, Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018)), which drive the difference in the predictive regression results. A complete understanding of the differences between various BAB portfolios requires a more comprehensive investigation, which we leave to future research.
Overall, testing the negative association betweenΔψand contemporaneous BAB returns yields strong statistical evidence in support of the theoretical prediction that the shadow cost of leverage constraints flattens the security market line (Garleanu and Pedersen (2011) and Frazzini and Pedersen (2014)). These results lend further support to the validity of usingψas a proxy for the shadow cost of leverage constraints.
As an important precursor to this paper, Boguth and Simutin (2018) test the leverage-constraint-based explanation of the BAB effect by using the market beta of active mutual funds' stock holdings (i.e.,βMF) as an LCT measure and find a strong positive predictive relation between the LCT measure and the BAB premium. In this subsection, we argue that there are theoretical reasons why the BAB premium can be positively or negatively correlated with the LCT measure (or other quantity measures of the LCT in general), depending on the relative importance of supply and demand shifts in the leverage market. Empirically, we test this prediction by using our shadow cost measure and the LCT measure jointly.
To understand the economic intuition, consider the model in Boguth and Simutin (2018) that extends Black (1972) to analyze an economy with both leverage constrained mutual funds and unconstrained hedge funds. Because active mutual funds face binding (regulatory or self-imposed) leverage constraints, if they want to achieve higher expected returns, they resort to purchasing high-beta stocks with implicit leverage from hedge funds. Hedge funds can bet against beta by selling high-beta stocks and buying low-beta stocks with explicit leverage, effectively supplying high-beta stocks (and thus implicit leverage) to mutual funds. Boguth and Simutin (2018) focus on the equilibrium driven by demand shifts. That is, holding hedge funds' supply of leverage fixed, higher demand for leverage from mutual funds leads to an increase in the LCT measure (βMF), a tightening of the leverage constraint, and an increase in the BAB premium. However, if the mutual funds' demand for leverage is fixed, a higher supply of leverage by hedge funds would lead to an increase in the LCT measure (βMF), but a loosening of the leverage constraint, and a decrease in the BAB premium.
To test this alternative prediction, we first follow the univariate regression specification in Panel A of TableIVusing the 12-month moving average of the LCT measure as the predictor rather thanψ. In columns (1) through (3) of TableVI, we find that the LCT measure does not predict the BAB returns with a significantly positive coefficient in our July 2006 to December 2016 sample.24This is in contrast to the positive association documented in the original sample in Boguth and Simutin (2018), suggesting that the relation between the LCT measure and the BAB premium can change signs.
Second, we find that in our sample period, our shadow cost measure (ψ) and the LCT measure tend to move in the opposite direction. Specifically, the Spearman rank correlation and the Pearson correlation between innovations in monthlyψand the LCT measure are−0.19(p-value=0.03) and−0.03(p-value=0.72), respectively. To the extent thatψis a price measure and the LCT measure is a quantity measure of equilibrium leverage, these negative correlations indicate that the supply-shift channel is on average more important in our sample period. This is perhaps due to the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis when the ability of financial intermediaries to provide funding is severely disrupted.
For robustness, in columns (7) through (9) of TableVI, we replaceCoMovewithSign, which is an indicator equal to 1 if the 12-month change in the level ofψand the LCT measure agree in sign and 0 otherwise. This allows for the possibility that high-frequency correlations may be zero while price and quantity comove over a long horizon. This specification yields stronger evidence for the supply- and demand-based prediction. The coefficients on the interaction term are all positive when predicting the benchmark BAB, EWβArb, and VWβArbportfolio returns, with bootstrapp-values of 0.04, 0.10, and 0.44, respectively.
Overall, our results suggest that the relation between the LCT measure and the BAB premium is time-varying. These findings connect to the burgeoning intermediary asset pricing literature, which increasingly recognizes that the relation between the level of leverage and the tightness of the leverage constraint depends on many factors. On the theoretical front, He and Krishnamurthy (2013) and Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014) argue that leverage ratios and the tightness of the leverage constraint are positively correlated if the equity constraint is binding; on the other hand, Brunnermeier (2009) and Adrian and Shin (2014) argue that the correlation can be negative if the debt constraint is binding. Empirically, researchers find that the sign of the correlation depends on whose leverage ratio is under consideration. For instance, He, Kelly, and Manela (2017) find that bank-holding companies' leverage ratios are higher when leverage constraints bind, while Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014) find that the leverage ratio of broker-dealers (i.e., subsidiaries of banks) are lower when leverage constraints bind. Adrian, Boyarchenko, and Shin (2015), Santos and Veronesi (2018), and Elenev, Landvoigt, and Van Nieuwerburgh (2018) offer empirical and theoretical analyses, showing that the correlation between leverage ratios and the tightness of leverage constraints depends on whether market or book leverage is used. Our results suggest that the correlation also depends on supply and demand shifts in the leverage market. Combining our shadow cost measure with existing leverage ratio measures can offer a new way to empirically investigate these effects.
article.VI. Relation to the Cross Section of Sto:
Our last set of tests examines hypothesis (iv), which states the shadow cost of leverage constraints drives the stochastic discount factor (SDF) that determines the cross section of stock returns. States of the world with high shadow cost of leverage constraints are states in which arbitrageurs' marginal utility of wealth is high. Consequently, stocks that pay off when the shadow cost is high are safe and should earn lower expected returns. We also highlight that a significant portion of the BAB premium can be explained by the BAB portfolio's exposure to the time variation in the shadow cost of leverage constraints.
We start the portfolio analysis with a univariate portfolio sort onβψ. At the end of each montht, all stocks in the given sample are sorted into quintile portfolios based on an ascending ordering ofβψ. We then calculate the value-weighted average montht+1excess return for each of the quintile portfolios, as well as for the zero-investment portfolio that is long the top quintile portfolio and short the bottom quintile portfolio (βψ5−1portfolio). All stock returns have been adjusted for the delisting returns in CRSP. The realized portfolio returns are available from July 2007 through December 2016.
Panel A of TableVIIreports the univariate portfolio sort results using all U.S.-based common stocks in the CRSP database that have a valid value ofβψat the end of each montht. We find that average excess returns are monotonically decreasing across theβψquintiles. The bottom-quintile portfolio generates an average excess return of1.11%per month, and the average excess return of the top-quintile portfolio is0.26%per month. The5−1portfolio average return of−0.85%per month is economically large and statistically significant with at-statistic of−2.5, after adjusting for heteroskedasticity and serial correlation following Newey and West (1987) with 12 lags.
To examine whether the pattern in the excess returns of theβψ-sorted portfolios is a result of exposure to previously identified risk factors, we calculate the abnormal returns of the quintile portfolios relative to the CAPM, the CAPM plus BAB model (CAPM+BAB), the Fama and French (1993) model (FF3), the Carhart (1997) model (FFC), the Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) Q-factor model (Q), and the Fama and French (2015) model (FF5).25The alphas exhibit a similar monotonically decreasing pattern across the quintiles. Theβψ5−1portfolio generates a monthly alpha of−0.94%(t-statistic=−2.8),−0.81%(t-statistic=−2.2),−0.81%(t-statistic=−2.6),−0.79%(t-statistic=−2.6),−0.76%(t-statistic=−2.2), and−0.55%(t-statistic=−1.5) relative to the CAPM, CAPM+BAB, FF3, FFC, Q, and FF5 factor models, respectively. The alpha of theβψ5−1portfolio relative to each of the factor models is negative and statistically significant. These results demonstrate that standard risk factors do not explain the relation betweenβψand average returns. Furthermore, to check whether the premium is driven by the crisis period, in Figure3, we plot the cumulative alphas of theβψ1−5portfolio . We find that the premium is dispersed as the cumulative alpha generally rises over time, with the only exception being the period around 2009, when the cumulative alpha declines.
Panel A of TableVIIalso reports the value-weighted preformation and postformationβψfor each of the quintile portfolios. As emphasized in Ang et al. (2006), “finding large spreads in the post-formation loadings is a very stringent requirement” in tests of a risk-based explanation that predicts contemporaneous relations between the true forward-looking factor loadings and expected returns. Because the true factor loadings are unobservable, researchers often implicitly assume that the portfolios sorted on the preformation sensitivities would have significant differential postformation exposure to the proposed risk factor. We examine the validity of this assumption by computing the postformationβψfrom regressing the entire time series of postformation portfolio daily excess returns on the contemporaneous MKT and innovations inψ, as in equation (16). The results in Panel A of TableVIIindicate that theβψ5 – 1 portfolio has a strong positive postformation sensitivity of 0.12, which is statistically significant at the 5% level. Thus, while the preformationβψis an imperfect measure of the true factor loading, it is sufficiently accurate to generate economically and statistically significant postformation exposure toψ.26
If the negative cross-sectional relation betweenβψand future stock returns is due to risk pricing, we would expect the relation to persist among liquid and large stocks. If instead, the negative relation stems from mispricing and limits to arbitrage, we would expect the relation to be weak or nonexistent among liquid and large stocks. To distinguish between the risk-pricing and mispricing explanations, we repeat the portfolio tests in the Liquid and Large Cap samples. The Liquid sample is the subset of the full sample with Amihud (2002) illiquidity (ILLIQ) values that are less than or equal to the 80thpercentile of the monthtILLIQ value among NYSE stocks. The Large Cap sample is the subset of the full sample with market capitalization (MKTCAP) values that are greater than or equal to the 50thpercentile value of the monthtMKTCAP among NYSE stocks.
We find that patterns in excess returns, factor model alphas, and postformation betas forβψ-sorted portfolios for the Liquid and Large Cap samples are very similar to those for the full sample. These results support the risk-based explanation for the cross-sectional relation betweenβψand stock returns. For brevity, we present the Large Cap sample results in Panel B of TableVIIand the Liquid sample results in TableA.XIIof the Internet Appendix. In the Large Cap sample, the average monthly return for theβψ5 – 1 portfolio is−0.70%(t-statistic=−2.6), which is comparable to the−0.85%per month average return on the5−1portfolio in the full sample. Standard risk factors once again cannot explain the cross-sectional relation betweenβψand stock returns. The5−1portfolio generates a monthly alpha of−0.82%(t-statistic=−3.6),−0.65%(t-statistic=−2.3),−0.71%(t-statistic=−3.2),−0.68%(t-statistic=−3.4),−0.67%(t-statistic=−3.0), and−0.52%(t-statistic=−1.9) relative to the CAPM, CAPM+BAB, FF3, FFC, Q, and FF5 factor models, respectively. Finally, consistent with the risk-based explanation for the pattern in returns, theβψ5 – 1 portfolio exhibits a significant post-formationβψof 0.11 (t-statistic=8.7).
For comparison, we conduct the same univariate portfolio sorting analysis based onβTED, which is computed under specification in equation (16) after replacingΔψwithΔTED. The results are presented in TableIA.XIIIof the Internet Appendix. In sharp contrast with the pricing effect ofβψ, we find no evidence that exposure to the innovations in the TED spread is priced in the cross section of stock returns. TheβTED5 – 1 average monthly excess returns are−0.18%(t-statistic=−0.6),−0.11%(t-statistic=−0.4), and−0.12%(t-statistic=−0.4) for the Full, Liquid and Large Cap samples, respectively. Adjusting for exposures to standard factor models yields alphas that are close to zero and statistically insignificant.
Having demonstrated a strong negative cross-sectional relation betweenβψand expected stock returns that is not explained by standard risk factors, we investigate the possibility that this relation can be explained by exposure to existing proxies for funding conditions. We compute the sensitivities of returns to these proxies and use them as the control variables. Specifically, at the end of each montht, we first sort all stocks into quintiles based on the ascending values of a control variable, and then within each control variable quintile, we sort stocks into quintile portfolios based on an ascending ordering ofβψ. We then calculate the value-weighted montht+1excess return for each of the resulting portfolios. Next, we compute the average montht+1excess return across the control variable quintile portfolios within eachβψquintile, and refer to this as the bivariateβψquintile portfolio excess return. Finally, we calculate the difference in montht+1returns between the bivariateβψtop- and bottom-quintile portfolios (bivariateβψ5−1portfolio). Since the bivariateβψquintile portfolios have similar values of the control variable, any return pattern across the bivariateβψ-quintile portfolios is unlikely to be driven by the control variable. Panel A of TableVIIIreports the equal-weighted average value of the control variable across the univariateβψ-quintile portfolios. Our Internet Appendix SectionIIdescribes the computation of these sensitivity measures.
First, we control for sensitivities to innovations in the TED spread (βTED). While Panel A of TableVIIIshows a positive monotonic relation betweenβTEDandβψ, Panel B of TableVIIIshows that controlling forβTEDdoes not appear to explain the negative relation between average returns andβψin the full sample. The bivariateβψ5 – 1 portfolio that is neutral toβTEDearns a monthly return spread of−0.62% (t-statistic=−2.2), CAPM alpha of−0.68% (t-statistic=−2.4), CAPM+BAB alpha of−0.61% (t-statistic=−2.2), FF3 alpha of−0.62% (t-statistic=−2.3), FFC alpha of−0.59% (t-statistic=−2.4), Q alpha of−0.53% (t-statistic=−2.0), and FF5 alpha of−0.45% (t-statistic=−1.6). Therefore, while controlling forβTEDreduces the return of theβψ5 – 1 portfolio, the risk-adjusted returns of theβψ5 – 1 portfolio remain economically large and significant.
Next, we control for VIX beta (βVIX), because (i) the time-series variation in VIX is correlated with fluctuations in hedge fund leverage ratios (Ang, Gorovyy, and van Inwegen (2011)) and (ii) beta exposure to time variation in the VIX negatively predicts stock returns (Ang et al. (2006)). Panel A of TableVIIIshows that the averageβVIXhas a nonmonotonic association withβψquintiles across the samples. Therefore, we do not expectβVIXto explain the pattern of returns acrossβψportfolios. In Panel B of TableVIII, we find that controlling forβVIXonly slightly reduces the excess returns on theβψ5 – 1 portfolio. The risk-adjusted returns all remain statistically significant.
In our next series of tests, we examine whether sensitivities to existing leverage measures in the literature can explain the premium associated withβψ. These measures includeβLCT(Boguth and Simutin (2018)),βLEV(Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014)), andβICR(He, Kelly, and Manela (2017)). Panel A of TableVIIIdoes not find monotonic patterns forβLCT,βLEV, andβICRacrossβψquintiles. These results are consistent with our discussion in SectionV.Cthat the relation between the level of leverage and the tightness of leverage constraints can be negative or positive depending on whether supply or demand shifts are driving the equilibrium. Panel B of TableVIIIfinds little evidence that any ofβLCT,βLEV, andβICRcan explain the pricing effect ofβψ. The average returns and alphas of the bivariateβψ5 – 1 portfolios that are neutral toβLCT,βLEV, andβICRrange from−0.41%per month to−0.92%per month, most of which are statistically significant at the5%level.
In the final column of Panel B in TableVIII, we control for idiosyncratic volatility (IVOL). Ang et al. (2006) find that IVOL is negatively related to the cross section of future stock returns. In Panel A of TableVIII, we see that stocks in both the bottom and topβψ-quintiles have similarly high IVOL. Therefore, we do not expect IVOL to explain the monotonically decreasing average returns acrossβψ-sorted portfolios. In Panel B of TableVIII, we find that the average return and alphas of the bivariateβψ5 – 1 portfolio remain economically large in magnitude after controlling for IVOL. All but the FF5 alpha are statistically significant at the 5% level.
We also examine the bivariate portfolio analysis using the Liquid and Large Cap samples and find similar results. These results are presented in TableIA.XIVof the Internet Appendix. Overall, the negative cross-sectional relation betweenβψand expected stock returns cannot be explained by stocks' sensitivities to existing proxies for funding conditions.
The magnitudes of the BAB premium and the shadow cost of leverage constraints are closely related in the models of Garleanu and Pedersen (2011) and Frazzini and Pedersen (2014). For instance, Proposition 2 in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) predicts that the BAB premium is the CAPM beta spread between the high- and low-beta portfolio times the shadow cost of leverage constraints,βH−βLβHβL×ψ. Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) note that “An interesting topic for future research is to empirically estimate agents' leverage constraints and risk preferences and study whetherthe magnitude of the BAB returns[emphasis added] is consistent with the model or should be viewed as a puzzle.” With ourψmeasure of the shadow cost of leverage constraints, we quantitatively assess this prediction.
As table 3 of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) shows, the average BAB return is0.70%per month and the realized CAPM betas for the low- and high-beta portfolios are 0.67 and 1.85, respectively, between 1926 and 2012. Following their Proposition 2, a back-of-envelope calculation implies that for the average shadow cost of leverage constraints to completely account for the BAB premium,ψshould be0.70%1.85−0.67×0.67×1.85=0.74%per month or8.82%per annum. Our estimation, however, shows that the average shadow cost of leverage constraints is only0.56%per annum between July 2006 and December 2016. Since our sample period is disproportionately affected by the 2009 financial crisis, the average shadow cost of leverage constraints in our sample period is likely already larger than the average over a longer sample period.
Reconciling the large BAB premium with an order-of-magnitude smaller shadow cost of leverage constraints presents a challenge to the hypothesis put forth by Proposition 2 of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014). One explanation explored by Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018) is that the original BAB factor constructed in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) overweights small-cap stocks that are expensive to trade, and thus, the true BAB premium is much smaller. According to Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018), net of transaction costs, the average BAB factor return, is almost 60% smaller. They further argue that after accounting for the BAB's exposure to the Fama and French (2015) five-factor model, less than 15% of the original BAB premium is left unexplained.
We offer a complementary explanation. If the shadow cost of leverage constraints is a key determinant of stock returns, as predicted by the leverage-constraint-based model of Frazzini and Pedersen (2014), then a natural implication of the model is that when the leverage constraint is more binding, high-beta stock prices rise as the implicit leverage in high-beta stocks becomes more valuable. Consequently, high-beta stocks would earn low returns not only because they help relax leverage constraints but also because they are a good hedge against the risk associated with time variation in the tightness of leverage constraints.
Following this logic, we explore how much of the BAB premium can be explained by its exposure to the time variation in the shadow cost of leverage constraints. Since the univariateβψ5 – 1 portfolio is a tradable factor-mimicking portfolio for the innovations inψ, we regress monthly BAB returns on contemporaneousβψ5 – 1 portfolio returns and the market excess return. We find that the loading on theβψ5 – 1 portfolio return is negative and significant (−0.17with at-statistic of−1.98), which is consistent with the view that the BAB portfolio is a hedge against an increase in the shadow cost of leverage constraints. Controlling for the exposure toψreduces the BAB portfolio's alpha to0.21%per month, which is 43% smaller than its CAPM alpha of0.37%per month. This reduction in alpha is statistically significant with a Newey-Westt-statistic of 3.05, suggesting that risk exposure to time variation in the shadow cost of leverage constraints is a quantitatively important factor in explaining the magnitude of the BAB premium.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Using the most comprehensive data set of daily leveraged funds known to the literature, we construct a new measure for the shadow cost of leverage constraints. The new measure offers two advantages compared to the widely used TED spread. First, leveraged funds are pure financial instruments for obtaining leverage. As we demonstrate, backing out their cost of leverage is straightforward, which provides a rare opportunity to infer the actual funding costs of leveraged institutional investors who borrow directly from major financial intermediaries. Second, unlike the TED spread, our shadow cost measure is less contaminated by credit risk because leveraged funds' borrowing has minimal credit risk.
We find that our shadow cost measure spikes upon quarter-ends, positively predicts the BAB premium, and negatively correlates with contemporaneous BAB returns. Furthermore, we find that innovations in our shadow cost measure are priced in the cross section of stock returns. Stocks that experience lower returns when our measure indicates a high shadow cost of leverage constraints on average earn 0.85% more per month. These time-series and cross-sectional patterns are consistent with the theoretical predictions of a valid proxy for the shadow cost of leverage constraints. In contrast, except for the negative relation with contemporaneous BAB returns, these patterns are not detected when we use the TED spread as the shadow cost measure.
Using our shadow cost measure, we also uncover evidence that calls for more research. First, we find that our shadow cost measure positively predicts the returns on the original BAB factor in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014) and the alternative equal-weighted BAB portfolios, but not the value-weighted BAB portfolios. This result, combined with the findings in Novy-Marx and Velikov (2018), suggests that firm size plays a role in the relation between leverage constraints and stock returns. Second, we show that a quantity measure of leverage constraint tightness can positively or negatively correlate with the BAB premium depending on the relative importance of supply and demand shifts in the leverage market. Finally, we find that the average shadow cost of leverage constraints is too small to explain the BAB premium, contrary to the prediction in Frazzini and Pedersen (2014). Overall, our results suggest that our new shadow cost measure is useful for assessing the asset pricing implications of leverage constraints.
